pages.string:
status: draft

# Charter

## Vision

Create a world creation engine for adventure games that is easy to incrementally contribute to without sacrificing the scope of what can be done.

## Mission

Using Rust and voxels

## User Roles

Hobbyist Worldbuilder

## Timeline

As this is a hobby-project, the timeline makes Epics at most a month in length.

-   June:
-   July:
-   August:
-   September:
-   October:
-   November:
-   December:

* * *

## Epics

As a hobbyist worldbuilder, I want a program that lets me visual voxel terrain segments in 3D quickly and easily so that I can experiment with building my own terrains procedurally.

-   Must be in Rust as the organization is trying to build expertise in Rust

## User Stories

As a hobbyist worldbuilder, I want to 

I want to be able to play with creating a bunch of heightmap terrains.

I want to be able to quickyl plug-in experimental modifiers on terrain building mechanisms.

I want to be able to write plug-ins and modify existing terrain algorithms with simple JavaScript scripts.

I want to be able to use WASM when contributing so that I have the benefits of Rust's tooling (which makes me a better engineer).

As a Fountain user, I want to have Similar Pages to have better matching on multiple words like "user story" because word pairs are often some of the more interesting connections.

* * *

## Notes

-   Statement
-   Refinement
-   Validation
    -   Written Principle
    -   External evidence

children.value: How to embed a bash script inside a Makefile
to ensure the multiline variable expands correct to the bash subshell

In this particular example, a bash script is used to wrap running multiple background 
processes together into logically a single Makefile command.

#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT
shutdown() {
    echo kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    exit 0
}
trap "shutdown" SIGINT SIGTERM

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--output-public-path dist \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	@bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"

pages.string:
tags: how-to

# How to embed a bash script inside a Makefile

## Overview

Relies on Make's features:

-   [`define`](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Multi_002dLine.html)
-   [`export`](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Variables_002fRecursion.html)

The basic concept is this:

-   Use `define` to specify a multiline variable
-   Use `export` to ensure the multiline variable expands correct to the bash subshell

## Example

In this particular example, a bash script is used to wrap running multiple background 
processes together into logically a single Makefile command. 

```makefile
#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT
shutdown() {
    echo kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    exit 0
}
trap "shutdown" SIGINT SIGTERM

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--output-public-path dist \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	@bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"
```

## References

-   <https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7281395/output-multiline-variable-to-a-file-with-gnu-make>
pages.title: How to embed a bash script inside a Makefile

children.value: In a changelog list, consider starting each bullet with one of the following verbs:
- something new has been added, prior functionality is unaffected
- some functionality still exists but has been modified in how it works
- some functionality still exists but is accessed in a different way
- some functionality that did not work correctly now works correctly
- content or data has been refreshed or added to in a backward compatible way

pages.string:
# Changelog Tips

In a changelog list, consider starting each bullet with one of the following verbs:

-   `Add` - something new has been added, prior functionality is unaffected
-   `Remove` - something has been removed entirely 
-   `Change` - some functionality still exists but has been modified in how it works
-   `Replace` - some functionality still exists but is accessed in a different way
-   `Fix` - some functionality that did not work correctly now works correctly
-   `Update` - content or data has been refreshed or added to in a backward compatible way
pages.title: Changelog Tips

-   As a developer, I want to see a set of passing unit tests so that I am higher confidence I can modify the code without causing regressions.

pages.string:
status: draft

# Product Development through User Stories

_There are many approaches to product development, this is just one possible take on it..._

## Goal

The goal of this **user story centered** approach is to create a lightweight approach that both helps guide effective day-to-day work but also covers strategic, year-long planning in a connected way.  

## Overview

This approach puts **TODO-WIKILINK** at the forefront of what is generated and iterated on. These are collected into fixed size **epics** so a timeline can be formed to allow for stakeholders planning around the projects.  As these initially evolve a short **project charter** is created to set a long-term north-star for the team. These work together to create a balance of customer value deliverability, predictiability, and iteration on what is learned along the way.

This approach has a bias towards scope-reduction over schedule or resourcing adjustment (in the traditional scoped-schedule-resources balance of product development).

## Checklist

_Skipping "the why" here's a checklist_

-   Brainstorm "I want..." statements
-   Refinement
    -   Put statements into "As a {user role}, I want {goal} so that {why}."
    -   Split and combine user stories, group into Epics
    -   Add acceptance criteria
-   Move into Backlog

## Getting started

This section assumes you're starting from scratch on this process. After working in this fashion for a while, it becomes a more natural, iterative processs of improving on the prior work: we'll get to that in the next section.

Assuming you're working with great people, they're probably not excited about learning a new process, but rather getting start to doing higher-quality work: this section tries to speed start to the value and layer in the process that matters as it goes.

### Product Owner

TODO: something about the decision-maker, moderator. Someone accountable to make decisions when not everyone agrees.

### Sketch the team's charter

TODO

### Generate a set of user stories

Expect the user stories to be varied and disorganized. The team is not yet aligned, so that's to be expected. Remember the following:

-   Anyone can write a story
-   The story should fit the format: _As a {user role}, I want to {goal or desire} so that {why}._
-   During brainstorming, do allow and record _I want to {goal}_: this can be refined later. Capture what people care about first.
-   These can be refined, grouped, and prioritized over time, but all are valid

### First pass of refining stories

TODO: Add user role

TODO: Split out **functional stories**

Pair each with an **acceptance criteria**, which is a set of 1-3 unambiguous questions that, when all can be answered yes, the work can be considered done. This is also called the "definition of done."  This piece is critical to enable people to scope work back to the minimal viable solution (and thus meet a value-delivery timeline).

-   The acceptance critiera fit the format: _The {specific detail} must {detail}, so that {why this matters}._

Aside: functional stories are not often part of Agile process. The reason being they don't deliver direct customer value, which is supposed to be the entire intent of product development, right? Having worked as a product manager at a highly technical company (where much of the company value was based on some expert-class engineering), I found ignoring the functional stories can remove engineers excellent skill in identifying opportunities, economic advantages. This ignores a major value add.

Try not confuse user stories or functional stories with tasks. If you have tasks that feel exciting, write them down but hold off on bringing them up until the planning phase.

### Refining user stories

This isn't a discrete step, but rather should happen as naturally as possible as part of either the generation or review of user stories.  User stories should not aim for perfection, but do look for ways to improve them:

-   Is the goal focused on the user value? If not, can the story be rewritten in a way that expressed why the user cares about this?
-   Does the story emphasize how this needs to be implemented? If yes, can that be removed and still express the user value?  If yes _but there's a functional requirement that really matters embedded there_, move that to the acceptance critiera.
-   Does is acceptance criteria short? If not, TODO
-   Does it have fuzzy words like "quickly" and "easily"? If yes, try to find ways to be more specific to avoid misaligned expectations and unclear goals.

### Group into epics

-   Decide on _maximum_ Epic length
-   Depending on team size, this may be best as 2 weeks, a month, or a quarter
-

TODO: estimation of user stories
TODO: estimation of epics

### Create a draft timeline

TODO: avoiding the word roadmap here

Lay out the order of the Epics

-   The team is making a commitment to pursue those Epics on that timeline
-   The commitment is to the value being pursued, not a set scope of implementation
-   The team's estimation are only estimations, not certainties

### Create a project charter

Keep it at 1-page.

-   Vision: why is this work being done? Is this inspiring?
-   Mission: How will this play out in rough terms that everyone can agree to
-   Success criteria: how & when are measurably successful
-   Timeline

Maybe a team charter?

## Epics

Questions, spikes.

Project charter (1-page)

-   Vision: why? inspiring
-   Mission: how? How will it play out
-   Success Criteria: 1 sentence tests
-   Timline: series of Epics?

## FAQ

### How do I keep the product cohesive? Everyone on my team has good ideas but they're all so different!

One approach - usually not best when first getting started - is bridging user stories before beginning work on them. Combine a succesffully completed user story and the new story:

_As a {user rule}, I use {product name} to {prior want} so {goal/why}.  I {new want} so that {goal/why}._

This can be helpful when existing user flows in the product may need attention due to their being incomplete, problematic for users, or there're opportunities to get more value from it. Generally if using this approach do not impose that the team must use a specific prior user story during the brainstorming process: this will stifle creativity. Instead rely on the charter to guide attention on the right areas.

### What do I do when the scope is enormous? (My team wants to do everything, the CEO has impossible demands, the minimal requirements for this to succeed are huge, etc.)

TODO

### Why use User Roles rather than personas?

User Roles intend to places more emphasis on the activities the person wants or needs to do. This avoids getting caught up in the job titles (which do not always map to the same responsibilities across companies) or irrelevant individual demographics biases.

### What do I do when the team chooses user stories that I don't agree with?

, I've been looking into leaning how to do multithreaded programming in Rust. The scenario I have in mind is to split the render buffer into tiles and let separate threads render each time, where the number of worker threads is a variable.
This requires some mechanism where a pool of threads is created, the tiles are dispatched to the threads awaiting work (i.e. some simple
), the tiles then rendered to a memory buffer, that then is passed back to the main thread to be copied into the main buffer.

Create a buffer for a 4k x 4k image
Iterate over  the image as a set of 128x128 tiles
    Assign the tile to over of the worker threads
    Have the thread fill in the tile's pixels

Stitch together the tiles as they are completed
Write the final buffer to disk

children.value: Build a pretty simple raytracer that:
Multithreaded Worker Queue in Rustct Browser rendering
Has a server interface for Browser API calls
Is documented, benchmarked, tested, and all the good things
Assumes you are comfortable with basic bash, Makefiles, and code editing
Assumes you have a recent version of Rust installed

We'll move quickly through this section of the challenge as the goal is writing the
raytracer, not setting up the Rust toolchain.

Before getting into anything too interesting, let's start with a basic program to fill a buffer
with values to get some Rust basics out of the way. We'll be approaching raytracing by looping
over a buffer of pixels and determining the color for each, so using let's use this basic
pattern to get something going first. It's often easier and more enjoyable to improve working
code than try get everything in there all at once.
Yes! Do this early, not after! Measure but don't optimize yet.

children.value: Data Objects are a way to specify a common class of data independent of how it is displayed.

Let's say you want to use Sea to store or share a series of recipes. The recipes may have a common data format and you don't want to duplicate the style and presentation for every individual recipe (and spend a lot of time trying to keep those in sync if you want any changes).
You could use the following block to represent a recipe:

```@recipe
name: Spaghetti Dinner
ingredients:
    - spaghetti
    - water
    - pasta sauce
steps:
    - boil water
    - heat pasta sauce
    - put spaghetti in water for 8-10 minutes
    - drain spaghetti
    - put spaghetti in a bowl
    - pour pasta sauce on the spaghetti
    - serve
```

value.title: Why YAML? Aren't there cleaner, better options?

children.value: YAML has its flaws, but is fairly common as a object-notation language.  It also is biased towards "ease of input" so is, arguably, a good match to the goals of Sea.
Future versions of Sea may support other data object specification languages via custom parsers as Sea is intended to be extensible in data specification.

pages.string:
# Data Objects

What are data objects?

Data Objects are a way to specify a common class of data independent of how it is displayed.

## Example

Let's say you want to use Sea to store or share a series of recipes. The recipes may have a common data format and you don't want to duplicate the style and presentation for every individual recipe (and spend a lot of time trying to keep those in sync if you want any changes).

You could use the following block to represent a recipe:

````yaml
```@recipe
name: Spaghetti Dinner
ingredients:
    - spaghetti
    - water
    - pasta sauce
steps:
    - boil water
    - heat pasta sauce
    - put spaghetti in water for 8-10 minutes
    - drain spaghetti
    - put spaghetti in a bowl
    - pour pasta sauce on the spaghetti
    - serve
```
````

#### Parsing

The `@recipe` label tells Sea to try the block that follows as a Data Object with the type `recipe`. Sea then parses the contents of the code section as YAML. This YAML then gets added to the Sea Tree as the parsed YAML data (rather than as a code block). 

#### Rendering

When the renderer comes across a Data Object it looks for a `Renderer` with the same name as the type of the Data Object. In this case, that means it looks for a `recipe.jsx` (or `Recipe` React Component).  This Renderer will be passed the YAML data to render the Node.

## Features

### Parsing fields as Sea content

An important use case to support is allowing individual field values to be parsed as Sea content.  Field names with a suffix of  `$[type]` are parsed as that type (unrecognized types are trimmed from the field name and ignored, per the principle that Sea should have reasonable default behavior for unavailable extensions):

    ```@two_columns
    columnA:  Hello **world**!
    columnB$sea:  Hello **world**!
    ```

In the above example, `columnA` will be parsed as plain text (the `**` will remain) and `columnB` will be parsed into an Sea Tree.

## Reference

### FAQ

```@expand
title: Why YAML? Aren't there cleaner, better options?
content$md: |
    YAML has its flaws, but is fairly common as a object-notation language.  It also is biased towards "ease of input" so is, arguably, a good match to the goals of Sea.

    Future versions of Sea may support other data object specification languages via custom parsers as Sea is intended to be extensible in data specification.
```

# Data Table Sometimes it may be helpful to have a one-off presentation of data for a specific page, where creating a global `Renderer` and broader in scope than needed. Here's an example. This is a list of tools/technologies/products that we want to create Google search links for each. Rather than typing out each, let's create a simple list of product names and some inline JSX to display that inline list. ### Results ```@data name=list, pizza=salad - Backblaze - Google One - Visual Studio Code - Blender - GIMP - Chrome ```

pages.string:
# Page Layouts

Sea is convention-based but tries to limit the number of conventions.

Pages are rendered as children of the optional `components/page_layout.jsx` file.  If no such file exists, there is no layout wrapper on the page content.

pages.string:
title: Sea
Work in progress page!
```@section style: marginTop: 64px textAlign: center content$md: | ![](sea_logo-v0.0.0.png) ```

Sea

#### What is Sea? Sea is an **extension of Markdown** designed for **data-driven organization** that can be used to build dynamic web sites and knowledge. Sea has the **fluency of Markdown** combined with the data-driven extensibility of **React** and **YAML**. It is designed for **incremental, progressive improvement** from idea to production-quality work. Think of it as an **agile content and presentation** language. It intentionally tries to be as **minimal** as possible while acheiving these goals. There's one basic tool: ```bash $ sea build dirname/ --out database.json # builds database.json ``` Then to host a site all that is needed is to copy `database.json` into a folder with a a base `index.html` and `sea-site.js`. Site customization exists entirely in the database itself, so there's no need to modify the host html or script. Note: this is a _single page static website_ so free hosts like GitHub Pages can easily host this. No operational burden! For active development, to launch a local server with hot reloading: ```bash $ sea-build dev . ``` How do I customize it? If you know basic JavaScript and React, it should be very easy: Sea tries to inject as few "framework specific" concepts as possible. Note: Sea is more of a _technology_ than it is a _product_. It's very flexible and customizable. Sea templates can overlay an opinion on the technology, creating a clear, immediately usable use cases - thus making Sea into more of a product. Can be thought of as a programming language. ## What problems does it solve? Semantic wiki, TiddlyWiki, MediaWiki, Markdown, Gatsby, database, spreadsheets, etc. Another swing at a balance of that functionality. Incremental development of ideas. Allow things to be "finished" and published sooner yet continually improved. ## Quick Start Install the `sea` command-line program: ```bash $ npm install --global @ridleywinters/sea ``` Create a barebones example project (note: if you skip the `--template` argument, a bare-minimal project will be created): ```console $ sea create myproject --template=example ``` You should now have a directory structure that looks something like this: myproject\ pages\ home.mdx page1.mdx Now run the development server: $ sea dev Lastly: - Visit http://localhost:8080! - Start modifying the MDX files to see the changes livee - Check out the [features](#features) section to play with some of the more interesting and unique features of Sea ## How does it work? Sea operates on a directory of files, typically stored in git (for personal knowledge bases) or in a database for large multi-user use cases. Each file represents a **Card** - a sttarting point, think of that as a fairly standard Markdown document. Sea parses the whole set of Cards and creates a tree of data with indices so that a renderer can then easily render the documents as well cross-reference information in other Cards. ## How does it compare? Sea is an extension of Markdown into multiple files to create a customizable database of information. It has the fluency of Markdown for simply writing text with minimal markup to support specifying data and formatting. It is customizable in that additional data types and renderers can be added to the database _directly_ as part of the database itself (no plug-ins, installation config, etc.). ```@expand title$md: How does it compare to [Notion](https://www.notion.so/)? content$md: | Leaner as it is command-line and file-based, it allows greater customization (it is easy to create custom "blocks"). However, it lacks the fluid UI, permissions model, and may other features that come out of the box with an app like Notion. ``` ```@expand title$md: How does it compare to [TiddlyWiki](https://tiddlywiki.com/)? content$md: | TODO! ``` ```@expand title: How does it compare to Evernote? content$md: | * Content is captured in files in git, giving full content and transparent on your data * No UI * No cross-platform syncing across multiple convenient apps * No features for quickly capturing data from other sources ``` ### Concepts - Data objects - Components (Renderers) - Parsers - Indices - Transformations (Graphs) - Exporters (Compilers) - Templates (?) - Style (?) Markdown with a couple extensions: - Data objects (YAML syntax) - Presentation objects (JSX syntax) Database: - All files parsed together and indexed for cross-referencing Server - Separate data from the presentation - API for operating on the data ## Publishing ### Publishing to GitHub Include an empty `.nojekyll` file in the root of your repository to [avoid having relative paths break](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31611969/broken-image-paths-on-github-pages-without-jekyll)! ## Musings In one sense, Sea is a literal programming language for iteratively moving from idea to implementation. It's one that "starts" with plain text. Markdown is then layered in to a structure to the text. Data objects and transformations can then be added in to programmatically turn inputs into new outputs. Exporters can then make these transformations available in external tools (whether that's a binary asset, a code package, etc.). ## Roadmap #### v0.1 - [ ] Create a command-line distributable with proper installation instructions - [ ] Create some basic documentation - [ ] Create a page to host it - [ ] Create a CHANGELOG #### v0.3 - [ ] Create a complementary web server - [ ] Create a flexible data source backend #### v0.4 - [ ] Create an API on the web server with edit commands - [ ] File write-back on API modifications - [ ] Page index of named objects ### v0.5 ### vX.X - [ ] Add templates - [ ] Add UI editors - [ ] Add a TableOfContents component - [ ] Rust / WASM support - [ ] Collaboration - [ ] Scale - [ ] Connecting pages into a book like mdbook ## Test Area ### Truly custom objects ```@section style: padding: '2px 8px' background: "rgba(20, 60, 200, 0.25)" content$md: | ### Hello world. * This is the beginning. * This is the middle. * This is the end. ``` ### Custom nodes The following creates a custom node in the AST of type `user_story`. The contents of the fenced section is parsed as YAML. ```@user_story story: As a user of this application, I want to specify custom objects in YAML so I can implement more visually appealing custom rendering. acceptance_criteria: - Must work - Must work well ``` By default, the renderer looks for a React renderer of the same name (in this case, `user_story.jsx`) and uses that to display the data object: ```@user_story story: As a user of this application, I want to specify custom objects in YAML so I can implement more visually appealing custom rendering. acceptance_criteria: - Must work - Must work well ``` ### Embedding specific data types This example uses the `$` suffix on a YAML field to indicate the parser that should be used on that field. In this case, it ensure the YAML field is parsed as Markdown (`$md`) so the `content` field is an AST as well. ```@expand title: This is an Expand object content$md: | This is a longer bit of content. I would prefer if this supported markdown and multiple lines, though. I need to consider some way to keep the AST going. * List Item A * Sub-item A1 * Sub-item A2 * List Item B * List Item C ``` ```@expand title: This is an Expand object content$md: | This is a longer bit of content. I would prefer if this supported markdown and multiple lines, though. I need to consider some way to keep the AST going. * List Item A * Sub-item A1 * Sub-item A2 * List Item B * List Item C ``` ## reference -

children.value: I started with Deno to avoid Babel configuration, but how to build client React code was not clear to me
Webpack complex-feeling configuration turned me to try parcel
Parcel does not seem to do hot loading or impl

npm i --save-dev webpack webpack-cli webpack-dev-server
npm i --save-dev babel-loader
so global installs are not needed
Want to hide this in a single
Make doesn't seem to directly support running multiple processes in the background as part of the command
Encapsulate the bash script to run multiple watchers in that long-string

#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT
shutdown() {
    echo kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    exit 0
}
trap "shutdown" SIGINT SIGTERM

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"

pages.string:
# Webpack Notes

## Goals, constraints, and background

-   Background
    -   I started with Deno to avoid Babel configuration, but how to build client React code was not clear to me
    -   Webpack complex-feeling configuration turned me to try parcel
    -   Parcel does not seem to do hot loading or impl
-   Goals
    -   Minimal configuration webpack development server

## Installation

```bash
npm i --save-dev webpack webpack-cli webpack-dev-server
npm i --save-dev babel-loader
```

```make
dev:
    npx webpack-dev-server
```

-   Using `npx` so global installs are not needed

## Babel configuration

In `webpack.config.js`:

```js
module.exports = {
    module: {
        rules: [
            {
                test: /\.(js|jsx)$/,
                exclude: /node_modules/,
                use: {
                    loader: "babel-loader"
                }
            }
        ]
    }
};
```

## Makefile configuration

-   Goals
    -   Want `nodemon` watching the database
    -   Want `webpack-dev-server` watching the source code
    -   Want to hide this in a single `make` command
-   Constraints
    -   Make doesn't seem to directly support running multiple processes in the background as part of the command

### Approach

-   Use `make`'s `define` command and `export` command to create a long-string
-   Encapsulate the bash script to run multiple watchers in that long-string

```make
#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT
shutdown() {
    echo kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    exit 0
}
trap "shutdown" SIGINT SIGTERM

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"
```
pages.title: Webpack Notes
